Note to Subscribers - Newsletter Delivery

I have published the daily digest of Best in Energy news and research every business day since 2014 and my own newsletter roughly three times a week since late 2024. The only exceptions are public holidays and about five weeks of annual leave each year. If am going on holiday or otherwise unable to publish for more than one day, I always notify subscribers in advance, usually via a caption at the top of the preceding daily digest. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me without warning, the most likely reason is your subscription has expired or your email system has begun to refuse delivery.

Distributing newsletters via email involves playing a constantly evolving cat-and-mouse game with corporate email security systems designed to counter spam, phishing and other malicious activity. The systems are set up to block and refuse delivery to anything that looks like spam and can be triggered by individual words in the text or simply the fact multiple people in the same organisation receive the same email at the same time. Every time spam filters are upgraded, which happens several times each year, there is a risk emails that were previously delivered safely will start to be refused.

Like other newsletter publishers, I use bulk mailing software (in my case a system called MailerLite). If your email system refuses delivery more than about half a dozen times consecutively, the mailing software will automatically remove your address from the mailing list with no input from me and no warning. This is an anti-spam measure and a legal requirement in most countries designed to prevent abuse.

To ensure uninterrupted delivery of the daily digest and my newsletters, please consider adding my email address (the one used to send this email john@jkempenergy.com) to your contacts or safe senders list. If you suddenly stop receiving emails from me, check your spam filter, speak with your corporate email department, or reach out to me and I will investigate. The problem of non-delivery can almost always be fixed but only once we are aware of it.

Very best wishes
John

text:
Cushing is not that important. 

I’m going to repeat again: it is NOT that important. 

PADD 2 gets constant crude flows from Canada. It is essentially a form of SPR. These Canadian heavy oil barrels have NOWHERE to go. They can only go to the US because Canadian politicians are dumber than a bag of cookies. 

And since US crude oil production is near 13.7 million b/d, the US is self sufficient in the barrel counting sense, but not the crude quality sense.

quotedTweet.text:
Take this from the guy that told you Cushing will tank bottom at the end of June. 

Cushing’s relevance to global oil prices wasn’t what it was like before. 

US has access to landlocked crude from Canada that goes directly to PADD 2. Couple that with 13.7 million b/d of US crude oil production and Cushing is really just a storage hub shifting barrels from one point to the next. 

However, the fact that the market is still pricing US barrels as if we have an abundance is wrong. We are reaching operational minimums, and the market should price the barrels out. 

But it is what it is.

German Defense Minister Boris Pistorius on Sunday blamed US President Donald Trump for the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, calling for the vital waterway to be reopened.

"Ultimately, the cork in the bottle neck of the Strait of Hormuz was pushed in by Donald Trump, not by us, but we have an interest in getting it out again," Pistorius said in an interview with broadcaster ARD.

"The opening of the Strait of Hormuz, or rather safe passage through it, is in Europe's interest, in the interest of our energy supply and our economic recovery," Pistorius added.

US efforts to open shipping channels in the Strait of Hormuz will allow a gradual increase in vessel transit, though security remains volatile, Chubb Ltd. Chief Executive Officer Evan Greenberg said.

“It’s from day to day, hour to hour,” Greenberg, whose company is a major insurer of commercial shipping, said on Fox News’ Sunday Morning Futures.

“Mines are the greatest uncertainty” in the strait, Greenberg said, as US and Iranian negotiators held talks in Switzerland on a permanent ceasefire and to lock in free passage through the waterway.

“We’re talking more about a war-zone environment,” he said. “Only a narrow channel is really being used to transit, and so it limits the number of ships that can actually go in and out. The Navy has been working to open up a broader set of channels, and as that happens, then shipping will increase.”

Copper inventories monitored by the three major futures exchanges fell by 54.7 kt last week to 1.088 million tons, extending the recent drawdown trend and reinforcing signs of tightening availability outside the United States. The decline was led by the SHFE, where stocks dropped 44.3 kt to 144 kt, the lowest level in six months, while LME-monitored inventories fell by a further 12 kt. In contrast, COMEX stocks rose by 1.6 kt, marking a tenth consecutive weekly increase as metal continues to be drawn into the US ahead of a potential copper tariff announcement expected by the end of June.

The divergence highlights the ongoing regional dislocation in the copper market. Traders and merchants have spent months redirecting material towards the US in anticipation of import restrictions, helping lift COMEX inventories to record levels while simultaneously draining stocks elsewhere. As a result, COMEX now accounts for 54% of all exchange-monitored copper inventories, a remarkable shift from historical norms, especially considering the nations relative small consumption level compared with the rest of the world.

Despite the recent increase in visible stocks, the broader market remains focused on a tightening medium-term outlook. The continued drawdown across Shanghai and London suggests underlying physical demand remains resilient, while concerns about mine supply growth, electrification demand, and the potential impact of US trade measures continue to support a constructive longer-term outlook for copper.

Over the past seven weeks, managed money's net long position in Brent crude has collapsed from a 7½-year high of 496k contracts to a 2026 low at 114k contracts in the reporting week ending 16 June, when prospects of a US-Iran peace deal gathered momentum and Brent subsequently tumbled 14%. The scale of the liquidation highlights how quickly speculative sentiment has swung from fears of a prolonged supply disruption to expectations of a sizeable supply surge once flows through the Strait of Hormuz normalise.

However, as highlighted previously, last week's 45% reduction in the net long was driven primarily by aggressive short selling rather than long liquidation. Gross short positions surged to 231k contracts, the highest level since the pandemic, while long exposure remained relatively elevated. This leaves the market increasingly vulnerable to a short-covering rally should the expected supply recovery prove slower than currently priced, or if traders are forced to reassess what given the present situation constitutes a fundamentally justified crude price.

Note weekly COT data from the CFTC will be published Monday due to Friday's Juneteenth holiday.

Wind Financial Morning Post: June 22, 2026

Market Brief

The week’s market focus centers on U.S.-Iran negotiations in Switzerland, with the Summer Davos Forum set to open.

Iranian negotiators say a draft exemption for Iranian oil sanctions has been finalized.

U.S. diplomats report progress in talks on navigation through the Strait of Hormuz.

Britain faces a political shakeup as Prime Minister Starmer may announce his resignation.

China’s Ministry of Transport forecasts over 652.78 million trips across regions during the Dragon Boat Festival holiday.

A surge in land prices has pushed floor prices for residential plots in Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen above 100,000 yuan per square meter.

Listed companies are ramping up share buybacks and insider purchases, with over 11 billion yuan in pledged buyback funds set to enter A-shares.

OpenAI has unveiled a long-term vision: every individual will have a personalized AGI assistant.

Top Stories

Major global events loom this week. Domestically, the June LPR rate will be announced on June 22; the 2026 Summer Davos Forum will be held in Dalian from June 23 to 25; the 2026 Shanghai Mobile World Congress opens on June 24; and China’s National Bureau of Statistics will release May industrial profits data. Internationally, progress in U.S.-Iran talks remains under intense scrutiny; U.S. May PCE price index and Q1 GDP final figures will be released; MSCI will announce its annual review, with South Korea potentially added to the developed markets watchlist; Nvidia’s shareholder meeting is scheduled; OpenAI’s GPT-5.6 models are expected to debut; and SK Hynix’s U.S. listing may be approved. Additionally, Micron Technology and others will release earnings.

On June 21 local time, the first round of U.S.-Iran negotiations in Switzerland concluded. According to Iranian sources, after 80 minutes of talks under the mediation of Pakistan and Qatar, the meeting was temporarily suspended for internal consultations. No discussion of Iran’s nuclear program took place during this round. The focus centered on implementing Article 13 of the memorandum of understanding, with priority given to issues related to Lebanon.

A member of Iran’s negotiating team revealed that a draft exemption for Iranian oil sanctions has been finalized and will be issued shortly. Administrative procedures for unfreezing Iran’s frozen assets are underway with participation from the Qatari delegation.

Reports indicate Iran’s negotiating team left the venue in protest over Trump’s threatening remarks. However, U.S. diplomats stated Iran’s delegation remains in Switzerland and negotiations continue, with intensive discussions ongoing on all elements of the nuclear agreement. 

Progress has been made on the issue of navigation through the Strait of Hormuz. Previously, U.S. President Trump posted on the social platform “Truth Social” that Iran must immediately halt its “proxy” activities in Lebanon, or face U.S. military action.

China Macro & Policy

China’s Ministry of Transport data shows that total interregional passenger volume during the three-day Dragon Boat Festival holiday (June 19–21, 2026) is estimated at 652.78 million trips, averaging 217.6 million per day, flat year-over-year. On June 21 alone, interregional passenger volume is projected at 220.2 million, up 9.4% from the previous day and 1.1% year-over-year.

China’s Ministry of Commerce data shows the holiday consumption market remained stable, with adequate supply and steady prices for essential goods. In the first two days of the holiday, foot traffic and revenue at 78 key pedestrian streets and commercial districts rose 4.0% and 3.5% year-over-year, respectively. Trade-in programs for consumer goods have also driven sales growth. As of June 20, 2026, trade-in initiatives have benefited 136 million people and spurred sales exceeding 1 trillion yuan.

On June 21, the 2026 APEC Business Leaders Forum was successfully held in Beijing. Ren Hongbin, Chairman of the China Council for the Promotion of International Trade and Chair of the 2026 APEC Business Leaders Summit, stated that Asia-Pacific business leaders must work together to uphold the multilateral trading system centered on the WTO, promote regional trade and investment liberalization, and deepen Asia-Pacific economic integration.

China Equities Markets

CITIC Securities said the AI-driven market rally this year has been fueled by massive infrastructure investment, resembling the 2006–07 bull market driven by capital-intensive industries rather than the dot-com bubble. Higher interest rates are unlikely to impact AI-related cyclical stocks unless they directly affect end-demand, commercialization assumptions, or capital expenditure growth. Globally, the initial impact of rate hikes is being felt most acutely in sectors with weaker demand growth, reinforcing a K-shaped divergence between AI and non-AI sectors.

Fund managers’ portfolio adjustments since the second quarter are becoming clearer, with divergent strategies emerging in pharmaceuticals, technology manufacturing, and consumer transportation. Significant differences exist in pharmaceuticals: traditional generic drugmakers are being sold off, while innovative drugs and active pharmaceutical ingredient-related stocks are being bought. Huaxia Fund’s Zhong Shuai has increased positions in packaging and testing firms; E Fund’s Qi He has added grid equipment stocks. E Fund’s Zhang Kun has reduced holdings in Huitai Medical and entered Guangshen Railway’s top ten shareholders.

As the SSE Index hovers around 4,100 points, A-share companies are launching a new round of market-stabilizing measures through share purchases and buybacks. Wind data shows that since June, 27 companies have disclosed shareholder purchase plans. More than 160 companies have announced buyback plans, with cumulative buyback amounts exceeding 11 billion yuan based on the lower limit and potentially surpassing 20 billion yuan based on the upper limit. Many firms have demonstrated strong execution.

Two new stocks will be offered this week. China Resources New Energy will open subscription on June 22, raising approximately 24.5 billion yuan — surpassing Joyson Electronics to become the largest IPO on the Shenzhen Stock Exchange. Yikun Electric will also begin subscription on the same day on the Beijing Stock Exchange.

China Industry News

A new wave of land price increases is sweeping through core cities. Floor prices for residential land in Beijing, Shanghai, and Shenzhen have all surpassed 100,000 yuan per square meter, with Shanghai’s highest land price exceeding 200,000 yuan per square meter last year. According to Centaline, in May, the total planned construction area of residential land sold in 300 cities fell 27.1% year-over-year, but average premiums rose to 9.5% due to strong demand for prime plots. In first-tier cities, land sales volume and proceeds rose 58% and 33% year-over-year, respectively, making them the most active segment of the land market.

According to online platform data, as of 9 p.m. on June 21, the 2026 Dragon Boat Festival box office reached 392 million yuan, with 10.59 million tickets sold and 1.44 million screenings — setting a new record for the highest number of screenings in Chinese box office history. Toy Story 5 led the holiday box office.

Prices for ferrophosphorus and lithium iron phosphate have surged this year, doubling from last year’s levels, yet demand remains robust. Strong exports of new energy vehicles and energy storage products are driving sustained demand growth, a key factor behind the price rise. Rising upstream raw material costs are another major contributor.

Public REITs in China have reached their five-year anniversary as of June 21. To date, 86 REITs have been listed nationwide with a total fundraising amount of 245 billion yuan, including 59 on the Shanghai Stock Exchange with 176 billion yuan raised. With the official launch of the first batch of commercial real estate REITs, the market now encompasses diversified asset classes including highways, industrial parks, energy, affordable housing, and commercial real estate.

The Guangxi Bureau of National Financial Regulation has issued multiple approvals permitting Guangxi Rural Commercial United Bank to acquire stakes in six rural credit institutions through subscription and transfer. Analysts note that Guangxi’s “upstream participation in downstream” reform is accelerating, signaling that provincial institutions are reshaping the governance structure of the regional rural credit system through equity linkages.

Fintech

The wave of AI infrastructure investment is reshaping global capital markets, but hidden debt risks cannot be ignored. Goldman Sachs forecasts that hyperscale cloud companies will spend $5.3 trillion on capital expenditures between 2025 and 2030. Morgan Stanley estimates that data center construction alone will require $2.9 trillion by 2028, much of it debt-financed. Meanwhile, corporate clients are beginning to rein in spending: Uber and Walmart have restricted AI usage, shifting pricing models from subscriptions to per-token billing, sharply increasing cost pressures.

Corporate News & Updates

OpenAI has unveiled a new long-term vision to bring advanced artificial intelligence to billions worldwide. The next phase of OpenAI’s development will focus on creating personalized AGI assistants to help individuals manage daily tasks, work, learning, and exploration.

OpenAI will launch its next-generation flagship model, GPT-5.6, expected to deliver transformative improvements in coding, agent workflows, and 3D generation, while offering lower pricing to directly challenge competitors. The GPT-5.6 series will include mini, standard, and Pro versions, with the most likely release window between June 22 and 28.

A supplier to Apple has begun small-batch deliveries for its first foldable iPhone. According to internal targets received by the supplier, the device is slated for release in September. A source at an Apple supplier confirmed that the target timeline is for the first foldable iPhone to launch in fall 2026. Apple has not publicly confirmed the release date.

Tesla has formally applied to register the trademark “Amazing Abundance,” signaling its deepening commitment to its long-term vision centered on artificial intelligence, humanoid robotics, and autonomous driving systems, with automation aimed at significantly reducing production costs.

Global Macro & Policy

Pakistan’s Foreign Minister Bilawal Bhutto Zardari, participating in a quadrilateral meeting with Egypt, Turkey, and Saudi Arabia, stated that within a 60-day period, Iran will not block vessels transiting the Strait of Hormuz, and no tolls or service fees will be imposed on passage through the strait.

UK Prime Minister Starmer is preparing to announce his departure timeline. After consultations with cabinet members, advisors, and unions, Starmer believes he can no longer continue in office. Senior Labour figures suggest he may issue a formal statement as early as June 22. Transport Secretary Heidemann urged Starmer on June 19 to set a clear timetable for his exit.

The Bank of Korea is concerned that massive bonuses at chip factories could trigger unexpected inflation. If wage hikes at semiconductor firms spread to other sectors, they could exert upward pressure on overall wages and intensify inflationary pressures through both supply and demand channels. South Korea’s May inflation rate reached 3.1%, the highest since early 2024. The central bank forecasts full-year inflation at 2.7%, significantly above its 2% target, making the July or September policy meetings potential windows for rate hikes.

Naka Matsuzawa, Chief Macro Strategist at Nomura Securities, believes the Fed’s June policy meeting will, in hindsight, be seen as a “once-in-a-decade turning point” — the start of a credit cycle inflection and the end of the AI boom. In his macro strategy weekly report, Matsuzawa noted that markets currently overestimate near-term Fed tightening risks but severely underestimate the risks of a longer-term tightening path.

Sources say Russia is considering channeling accumulated rouble surpluses from its rouble-rupee bilateral trade agreement with India into Indian infrastructure projects. Moscow plans to deploy these excess roubles into high-growth sectors including ports, biotechnology, and agriculture, as well as India’s special economic zones — aiming for long-term returns while hedging against Western sanctions.

Sweden’s largest power company, Vattenfall, announced it has selected Rolls-Royce to build three small modular reactors in southwestern Sweden, marking the country’s first nuclear reactor construction in over 40 years. Sweden and other Nordic nations are experiencing a resurgence in nuclear development amid energy supply disruptions, seeking stable and clean power sources.

Global Equity Markets

Encouraged by the U.S.-Iran memorandum of understanding and prospects for conflict de-escalation, U.S. equities rose last week, with major indices hitting new highs driven by tech stocks, though volatility persisted. Federal funds futures pricing shows a 70% probability of a rate hike by October. This week’s earnings report from Micron Technology may determine the near-term direction of the tech sector.

Bonds

Charles Hare, Chairman of the Policy and Resources Committee at the City of London Corporation, said current Sino-British financial cooperation focuses on green finance and offshore yuan. Through the China-UK Economic and Financial Dialogue, both sides have established working groups on green finance and yuan, jointly advancing green bonds and voluntary carbon market standards. He expressed hope that future cooperation will deepen liquidity in yuan markets and expand green financial products.

Commodities

Guinea’s President Mamadi Doumbouya has banned the export of raw gold to promote domestic processing and economic development. Speaking to local miners and gold buyers, Doumbouya stated that all gold must be processed within Guinea and raw ore will no longer be permitted to leave the country.

Global copper traders await the U.S. Department of Commerce’s review report, which will be pivotal in determining Trump’s final tariff decision. Morgan Stanley analysts estimate a 43% probability of a 15% tariff on refined copper taking effect in January. Given copper’s irreplaceable role in AI infrastructure, most analysts and traders remain broadly optimistic about copper’s long-term outlook.

Key Economic Events

08:00 EDT, Jun 22: Canada National Economic Confidence Index through Jun 19
08:30 EDT, Jun 22: Canada CPI for May
10:00 EDT, Jun 22: Eurozone Consumer Confidence Index preliminary for June
21:20 EDT, Jun 21: The People's Bank of China has CNY 818 billion in 7-day reverse repos maturing
05:00 EDT, Jun 22: ECB policymaker Kocherlakota to speak
08:30 EDT, Jun 22: ECB President Lagarde to speak at the European Parliament
SK Hynix plans to list in the U.S., and the U.S. SEC may approve as early as the week of Jun 22
"Digital-Smart Sichuan" industrial chain international cooperation exchange conference to be held on Jun 22
The 4th Chain Expo to be held from Jun 22 to 26
Roundtable on cooperation of the Land-Sea New Channel International Chamber of Commerce to be held on Jun 22

Sunday Market Update - Crude Oil Outlook - June 21, 2026

NOTE: News Headlines Can Change This Outlook Before Market Open. - Oil Remains News Sensitive....

Top News Story: Volatility Rocks Crude as JD Vance's Comments Spark Fresh Doubts Over U.S.-Iran Peace Durability

Crude oil prices are seeing a highly volatile reset as the market slides down into a new trading channel, with WTI settling near $75.50/bbl following consecutive weeks of heavy liquidation. 

While an interim U.S.-Iran diplomatic truce has allowed some long-stranded physical vessels to begin moving through the Strait of Hormuz, optimism hit a major roadblock over the weekend. 

Comments from Vice President JD Vance raised serious questions regarding the stability and long term durability of the peace agreement, injecting fresh geopolitical anxiety back into the complex just as traders expected a smooth return of waterborne logistics. 

Compounding this friction, reports over the weekend indicate that Tehran is threatening to slow the implementation of the waterway reopening unless specific regional ceasefires are fully respected and critical oil export waivers are formally signed. Automated trading programs have rapidly shifted behavior, abandoning the straight-line capitulation dump to brace for a highly fragmented, headline-sensitive trading band.

Market Status: DISRUPTED TRUCE / CHANNEL CONSOLIDATION

Global Price Benchmarks (As of Friday Close)
Brent Crude: $79.33 (Closed Friday) | -0.65% on the day, marking its first slip below the psychological $80 threshold since early spring

WTI (US): $75.50 (Closed Friday) | -1.15% on the day, testing support parameters near the recent swing lows after a sharp weekly pullback

Gasoline (RBOB): $2.44/gal (National Avg) | Pulling back significantly from early summer projections as crude input costs deflate

Diesel: $2.31/gal (Heating Oil/Distillate) | Retracting toward pre-crisis footprints, offering substantial breathing room to continental logistics networks

The Headline-Driven Era: Trading the "Tape"
The tape has quickly transitioned from a straight downward flush into a choppy, news-driven battleground where political commentary dictates algorithmic order execution.

Asymmetric Risk: Path of least resistance has turned neutral-to-bearish for near-term contracts. Algorithmic systems are aggressively fading minor intraday bounces, keeping a heavy lid on prompt delivery pricing until formal signatures are finalized.

The "Zero Liquidity" Trap: Depth of book remains highly fragile over the weekend open. Wide bid-ask spreads mean any sudden pushback or conflicting statements from diplomatic envoys regarding the deal's exact terms will trigger immediate $3–$4 vacuum spikes.

Verification Dynamics: Traders are trapped in a highly reactive news cycle. Automated systems are executing purely on raw political feeds, leaving physical inventory fundamentals secondary to the micro-level diplomatic tape.

Social Media Buzz: The View from X
Energy networks are experiencing total volatility as commentators dissect the conflicting political reports surrounding the permanence of the maritime accord.

(Oil On The Tracks): Satellite tracking accounts are working overtime, noting that while some idled tanker capacity has begun to clear, a massive concentration of vessels remains anchored outside the chokepoint waiting for official clear signals.

The Technical Breakdown: Chart-focused accounts are sounding the alarm, noting that WTI has established thick overhead resistance near the $79.20 zone, validating a broader consolidation channel.

Agricultural Relief: Farm and agribusiness handles are highlighting the ongoing slide in fuel futures, noting the immediate cost relief this localized flush continues to deliver for mid-summer regional operating budgets.

Macro Volatility: Speculation is building that if energy inputs remain suppressed within this lower band, central banks will have a clear runway to revive deferred interest rate cuts before the end of the summer.

Shipment & Logistics Tracker: Monitoring Critical Choke Points

Strait of Hormuz: FRAGILE TRANSIT STATUS (Impact: High) The absolute physical blockade has fractured, but strict administrative bottlenecks remain as naval task forces implement cautious clearance protocols.

Suez Canal: CONDITIONAL RE-ENTRY (Impact: High) Fleet operators are closely analyzing the weekend political friction, keeping container routing adjustments tentative until long-term security parameters are fully validated.

Cape of Good Hope: TON-MILE COOLING (Impact: Moderate) Extreme maritime premiums are cooling off as standard shipping networks prepare for a gradual migration back toward northern arteries, softening global transit penalties.

Panama Canal: PRESSURE BALANCING (Impact: Low) Wait times are stabilizing as global freight routing expectations normalize away from emergency transshipment detours.

Supply & Production Intelligence
Stockpile Dynamics: Goldman Sachs highlights that even as oil flows slowly begin to normalize, global country reserves are heavily depleted from the spring crisis, which will require steady restocking through late 2026.

OPEC+ Tracker: The market is realizing that scheduled voluntary production increases will now land on an opening, expanding spot supply chain, further dampening prompt pricing.

Secondary Shocks: Severe deflation in input costs is flowing through to fertilizer and energy-heavy manufacturing components, lowering inflation indicators.

Weekly Performance Snapshot (June 15 Open to June 19 Close)

Frontline (FRO): $27.10 | -13.28% (Tanker equities continue to drop sharply as long-haul ton-mile demand forecasts collapse on returning routes)

ZIM Integrated (ZIM): $19.55 | -10.73% (Ocean freight premiums deflating further on the back of looming chokepoint resolutions)

Valero (VLO): $238.40 | -1.53% (Refining stocks sliding as refining margins compress on cooling prompt crude prices)

Chevron (CVX): $179.20 | -1.08% (Losing safe-haven asset allocations as capital rotates into broader risk-on equity sectors)

Enbridge (ENB): $54.30 | -1.45% (Experiencing mild contraction in sympathy with the broader energy sector reset)

Price Summary and Recent Movements
WTI has officially broken its multi-month structural uptrend, erasing nearly all premium metrics accumulated since late February. The market structure has sharply adjusted, transitioning from extreme backwardation to a flatter, highly stable pricing curve as refiners pause immediate prompt-month bidding wars.

NYMEX Data (WTI/bbl): The prompt contract closed at a definitive $75.50/bbl on Friday. Crucially, the steep backwardation that defined the spring market is collapsing rapidly, with prompt delivery premiums flattening as the immediate scramble for waterborne inventory cools.

Outlook for the Week Ahead
The technical and fundamental bias is geared toward channel consolidation in the near term, entirely tethered to the formal signing and execution of the chokepoint reopening. 

Traders must manage severe headline gap risk over the weekend session open.

Strategy: The previous psychological support floor at $82.00 has transformed into severe overhead resistance. With WTI closing at $75.50, the next major structural downside technical targets sit at the $72.50 support cluster, with a deeper macro invalidation target pointing to the high-$68s if the deal crosses the finish line.

Global oil prices have eased after Brent crude fell back below $78 per barrel following the easing of US-Iran tensions in the Strait of Hormuz, but fuel prices across several African countries remain stubbornly high. Malawi recorded the steepest increase, with prices rising from $2.02 to $3.83 per litre between January and May. Zimbabwe climbed from $1.57 to $2.08, while Cabo Verde, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, and Tanzania also ranked among the continent's costliest fuel markets. In Nigeria, petrol has surged past ₦1,336 per litre despite recent declines at the refinery level. Analysts attribute the disconnect to three factors: heavy reliance on imported refined fuel, weak local currencies that inflate dollar-priced imports, and pricing structures that adjust quickly during price spikes but lag during declines. Until currency stability improves and import dependence eases, African consumers are unlikely to see proportional relief at the pump. Source: Business Insider Africa

📊 Economic Data Preview: A Lighter Week Ahead (June 22–26, 2026)

As markets enter a notably quieter stretch of the economic calendar, investors have a clearer window to digest fresh readings on growth, inflation, labor conditions, and consumer behavior.

Monday, June 22 & Tuesday, June 23 No major U.S. economic releases are scheduled. This breathing room allows focus to shift toward positioning ahead of the data-heavy midweek.

Wednesday, June 24
Current Account Balance
EIA Crude Oil Inventories
MBA Mortgage Applications Index
New Home Sales
These figures will offer timely insights into external balances, energy supply dynamics, housing affordability and demand, and mortgage market activity, all sensitive to interest rate expectations.

Thursday, June 25 (Highlight Day)
Initial & Continuing Jobless Claims
Durable Goods Orders
EIA Natural Gas Inventories
Q2 GDP – Third Estimate
PCE Price Index (headline & core)
Personal Income & Spending
Thursday stands out as the most consequential session. The final GDP revision will refine our picture of economic expansion, while the PCE readings, the Federal Reserve’s preferred inflation gauge, are likely to draw the sharpest attention for clues on price pressures and future policy direction. Labor market data and spending figures will round out the assessment of underlying momentum.

Friday, June 26
Advanced International Trade in Goods
Advanced Retail & Wholesale Inventories
University of Michigan Consumer Sentiment (final)

The week closes with forward-looking consumer confidence data alongside supply-chain and inventory metrics. Sentiment readings often preview shifts in household spending, which remains the backbone of U.S. economic activity.

With key growth, inflation, and confidence indicators on the docket, this data cluster could help shape near-term expectations for monetary policy and market sentiment heading into the summer.

Week-Ahead Brief | June 22-26 2026 | SPX 7500.58 close (Thursday OPEX)

The cheapest options of the year going into the biggest macro week. Five days mapped. Daily levels and scenarios.

Goldman warned Friday that this week looks calm but isn't. Here is why.

Three signals are all pricing the same thing. Monday options trade at 8.91 percent vol, the cheapest pricing since Christmas holiday week. COR1M sits at 8.02. Below 8 is extreme. When correlation snaps back, the move is usually violent. June OPEX cleared Thursday with more call positioning than puts, leaving dealer hedges to unwind Monday and Tuesday. Three asymmetric signals stacked into a week with Core PCE Thursday.

The week builds straight into PCE. Monday Fed Waller speech 9 AM kicks off post-FOMC commentary. Tuesday PMI Flash gives the first economic activity read. Wednesday brings Stress Test results plus Micron earnings after-hours. Thursday Core PCE is the headline event. Friday closes Q2 with pension rebalancing flow last 30 minutes.

Iran closed Hormuz again Saturday as talks open in Switzerland Sunday with Lebanon attacks still live. Watch Brent at the Monday open for the inflation read.

Thursday closed at 7500.58 and took back the pivot. 7500 anchors the week. 7440 to 7480 EMA confluence holds the floor. Above 7600 the post-OPEX residual call wall caps the upside. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

THE WEEK AT A GLANCE

Mon 6/22 (CAUTION flag): Fed Waller speech 9 AM. Post-OPEX dealer unwind window starts. Light data day. Bill auctions only.

Fri 6/26 (CALM flag): Goods Trade Balance 8:30 AM. Michigan Sentiment Final 10 AM. Baker Hughes Rig Count 1 PM. Last full trading day of Q2. Quarter End rebalancing flow into the close.

THE VOL PICTURE

Vol is priced for a quiet week into PCE Thursday. That pricing is the asymmetric part of the setup.

Monday at-the-money options trade at 8.91 percent IV. Goldman flagged that level approaches the 7 percent lower bound seen during the Christmas holiday week. That is holiday-week pricing for a week that has Fed Waller speaking Monday morning, PMI Flash Tuesday, Stress Test results Wednesday, Core PCE Thursday, and quarter-end Friday. The options market is paying almost nothing for protection on Monday and Tuesday.

The straddles tell the same story. Monday options price an 85 point expected move from the close. Friday options price 110 points. The 25 point gap is the entire week of risk premium. Most of it lives in Thursday's PCE print and Friday's month-end flow.

Goldman also flagged the structural imbalance from Thursday's OPEX. The June expiry cycle had more call delta notional than puts. When those positions roll off, dealer hedges associated with them unwind, and that can contribute to elevated volatility. The institutional research desks are openly warning about Monday and Tuesday being trap days.

VVIX sits at 88.43, right at its historical mean of 85. After running to 94.53 Wednesday on the hawkish dot plot, vol-of-vol normalized into Thursday's OPEX rally. Going into a PCE week with the dot plot now showing rate hikes as a real possibility, VVIX at the historical baseline is not paying up for protection. That mirrors what the index options market is doing.

The vol curve says the market thinks Monday and Tuesday are sleepy then vol builds into PCE Thursday and quarter-end Friday. If post-OPEX dealer unwind hits as Goldman called out, that assumption breaks fast and Monday or Tuesday becomes the surprise vol expansion.

WHAT HAPPENED THURSDAY OPEX

SPX closed 7500.58, up 80.48 points. Opened 7487.36. Session low 7468.32. Session high 7511.07. Reclaimed the 7500 pivot that broke Wednesday post-FOMC. Closed RIGHT AT the level. Range only 42.75 points which is tight for an OPEX day. Vol absorbed cleanly.

Dow +0.14 percent. Russell 2000 +2.12 percent (smallcaps led). Nasdaq +1.91 percent on Intel-Apple chip news. Brent oil dropped on Iran deal optimism then bounced back to $80 Friday on Hormuz uncertainty.

AAII Sentiment for week ending 6/17 showed Bullish 36.6 percent, Bearish 39.4 percent. More bearish than bullish, more bearish than the historical average of 31 percent. That is a contrarian-bullish positioning setup, especially with smallcaps showing the strongest sentiment in the special question at 27.4 percent.

The Sep 15 Fed meeting now prices 50 percent No Change and 38 percent 25 bps Hike. Before Wednesday the hike probability was near zero. The hawkish dot plot repriced the curve. PCE Thursday is the next test.

THE WEEK'S BIG STORY

Four threads pull on this week.

1. Post-OPEX dealer unwind Monday and Tuesday. Goldman flagged June OPEX had more call delta notional than puts. When call hedges unwind without put hedges balancing them, the unwind tilts the tape. Vol can expand from compressed levels fast.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Core PCE is the Fed's preferred inflation gauge. After a hawkish dot plot now showing 38 percent hike probability at September, any upside surprise puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Below 7400 is the structural risk zone if PCE comes in hot.

3. Iran 60-day clock and Hormuz. Iran declared Hormuz closed Saturday, calling it the IRGC's first operational step. US-Iran talks open Sunday in Switzerland. Markets have so far dismissed the closure as rhetoric since 55 ships transited Hormuz Saturday despite the announcement. But Brent at $80 and Israel still attacking Lebanon mean energy can spike Monday open if Sunday talks fail. The peace deal is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday. Pension rebalancing flow possible last 30 minutes of the session. Q2 ends Friday June 26 since June 30 is a Tuesday. Watch closing tape.

The 7320 and 7310 levels look like a paired position - support catches at 7320 with acceleration just below at 7310. Likely an insurance trade ahead of PCE. Watch how it develops.

If PCE comes in line, vol compresses and we drift back toward the 7500 anchor.
If PCE comes hot (above expectations), 7450 is first catch and 7400 magnet pulls.
If PCE comes cool, 7565 and 7600 zone opens for a relief rally.

FRIDAY JUNE 26 PREVIEW - QUARTER END

CALM flag. Last full trading day of Q2. Quarter End rebalancing flow into the close.

Key catalysts: Goods Trade Balance 8:30 AM. Wholesale and Retail Inventories. Michigan Sentiment Final and 5-Year Inflation Expectations 10 AM. Baker Hughes Rig Count 1 PM.

Straddle: +/- 110.15 ref 7500.58 (7390 to 7611) - widest of the week

Key levels for Friday:
7550 sellers stacked heavy (-552 / -624) = fuel up zone
7545 sellers stacked
7535 light fuel up with put support
7500 anchor with put support rebuilt
7485 strong support
7460 drops fast if it cracks
7430 strong support
7425 strong support, well defended
7400 strong support
7375 drops fast if it cracks
7340 strong support · deep catch

Quarter-end positioning typically tilts directional based on equity performance Q2. If Q2 ends positive (which is likely given Thursday's recovery), pension rebalancing buys bonds and sells equities the last 30 minutes. Watch closing tape closely. Could be the most directional last 30 minutes of the month.

THE WEEK'S RISKS

1. Goldman's post-OPEX dealer unwind warning Monday and Tuesday. Vol priced for calm. The map shows 7660 and 7650 call walls from OPEX residual on Monday that vanish Tuesday. The transition between days is where vol can fire.

2. PCE Thursday is the gravity center. Hawkish dot plot showed 38 percent hike probability at September meeting. Hot PCE puts hike pricing above 50 percent. Cool PCE relieves the entire curve.

3. Iran Hormuz closure and Lebanon. Talks open Switzerland Sunday. If they fail or stall, Brent pops Monday open and feeds the inflation story into PCE Thursday. 55 ships still transited Hormuz Saturday so the immediate impact is rhetoric not reality. But the situation is fragile.

4. Quarter-end Friday pension rebalancing. If Q2 ends positive, expect equity selling and bond buying last 30 minutes. Could be a setup for a Monday June 29 reversal if positioning unwinds.

RULES

1. Vol is priced at holiday-week levels. The setup is asymmetric. Trade smaller until the post-OPEX unwind window clears.

2. 7500 is the anchor for the entire week. Hold it Monday and bulls have a path. Lose it and the structure changes.

3. 7440 to 7480 is the moving average confluence zone. 20D EMA 7441.01 + 20D SMA 7481.88. The strongest technical support cluster of the week sits here.

4. Goldman warned about post-OPEX dealer unwind hitting Monday and Tuesday. Vol can expand fast from compressed levels. Treat all levels as breakable Monday and Tuesday.

5. The 7345 acceleration zone on Monday's put map is the worst-case break. Massive sellers stacked. If 7350 fails, the move accelerates.

6. PCE Thursday is the headline event. Position smaller into the print. The 8:30 AM data drop includes PCE + Durable Goods + GDP + Jobless Claims all at once. Most catalyst-heavy moment of the year so far.

7. The 7320 and 7310 paired levels on Thursday's book look like an insurance trade. Watch how they develop into PCE.

8. Friday is quarter-end. Last 30 minutes can be one-directional based on Q2 performance. Stay flat or hedge into the close.

9. Iran headlines from Sunday's Switzerland talks set the Monday open tone. If Brent pops, the inflation story for PCE Thursday gets louder.

10. Above 7500 we stay bullish into PCE. Below 7400 we stay bearish into PCE. The middle is the chop zone where most of the week lives.

Not financial advice. Verify all levels before trading.

🚨Iran claims it “closed” the Strait of Hormuz.

Yet millions of barrels of oil are still moving through the world’s most critical chokepoint.

Let’s be clear: if Hormuz were actually shut, crude wouldn’t be chilling. You’d see an immediate spike in oil, tanker rates, insurance premiums, and volatility across risk assets.

Instead? Flows continue.

This tells you the market views this as narrative escalation — not a physical supply disruption.

Remember: ~20% of global oil passes through Hormuz. A real closure isn’t a headline event. It’s a global macro shock.

Until barrels stop moving, this is geopolitics theater.

Others are selling fear or farming engagement. I focus on flows, positioning, and real market impact. If you’re not following, you’re missing the alpha 🛢️📊
